data:
Search results for "Scheme interpreter eval apply mutual recursion self-evaluating expressions":

Title: Project 4 - Scheme Interpreter - CS61A Summer 2019 - GitBook
URL: https://tomthestrom.gitbook.io/cs61a-summer-2019/cs61a-summer-2019/project-4-scheme-interpreter
Content: # Project 4 - Scheme Interpreter. In this project we built an interpreter for a subset of the Scheme language. I think it's useful for students new to programming to work on a project like this, because it requires them to navigate a "larger" codebase as opposed to the atomic problems that one deals with in the homeworks and labs. Here is a brief overview of each of the Read-Eval-Print Loop components in our interpreter. * **Read**: This step parses user input (a string of Scheme code) into our interpreter's internal Python representation of Scheme expressions (e.g. Pairs). You do not need to read or understand the code for this step. Together, these mutually recursive functions parse Scheme tokens into our interpreter's internal Python representation of Scheme expressions. Code for this step is in the main `scheme.py` file. If the procedure is a user-defined procedure, `scheme_apply` creates a new call frame and calls `eval_all` on the body of the procedure, resulting in a mutually recursive eval-apply loop.

Title: Project 4: Scheme Interpreter - BYU CS 111
URL: https://cs111.byu.edu/archives/sp23/proj/scheme
Content: # Project 4: Scheme Interpreter. > * submit with Part II & III (including passing all tests provided in `tests.scm`) by **Friday 16 June** (worth 1 pt), and. In this project, you will develop an interpreter for a subset of the Scheme language. As you proceed, think about the issues that arise in the design of a programming language; many quirks of languages are byproducts of implementation decisions in interpreters and compilers. The subset of the language used in this project is described in the functional programming section of Composing Programs, as well as this language specification and built-in procedure reference for the subset of Scheme that you’ll be building in this project. You can download all of the project code as a zip archive. * `scheme_eval_apply.py`: the recursive evaluator for Scheme expressions. * `scheme_reader.py`: the reader for Scheme input (this file is obfuscated so that you can implement it in lab).

Title: An Interpreter with let and lambda - Scheme Docs
URL: https://docs.scheme.org/schintro/schintro_123.html
Content: We'll just use recursion to evaluate subexpressions, and rely on the language we're implementing the interpreter call eval recursively to evaluate

Title: 4.1 The Metacircular Evaluator
URL: https://sarabander.github.io/sicp/html/4_002e1.xhtml
Content: For self-evaluating expressions, such as numbers, eval returns the expression itself. Eval must look up variables in the environment to find their values.

Title: That author has a bit of a misunderstanding about Lisp
URL: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=42845906
Content: "Lisp evaluation works by mutual recursion of eval and apply. Eval looks at an expression and if it is a function application it calls apply. Apply in turn
